description: Thursday 06 November 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee Briefing on meetings with non-governmental organisations and individuals Hearing of evidence Submitter list: Dr Terence Wood
descriptionSummary: Briefing on meetings with non-governmental organisations and individuals. Dr Terence Wood

chunks.text: Pacific. And we've had a number of questions, uh, for MFA over the course of the last few months on this, of the back of hearing from a group thought, the Pacific Exporters Network earlier in the year. And we were interested in how the aid was flown to Pacific, how we're tracking that in terms of, um, the Zealand business involvement in that in particular.

And uh, your name came up as, uh, someone with a high level of, uh, knowledge and expertise in this area. And so we wanted to extend an invitation to you, Dr. Wood, to come join us, to give us some insight around the flow of aid from your perspective to help our understanding. Um, we have 15 minutes sit down for today.

Hopefully that's all right for you. I'm sure there'll be questions from people as well. Uh, but we're really interested in, I guess, uh, a bit of a summary of the volume of aid, where it's going, who's doing it, and a tight untied aspects. Um, and then we can delve into questions from there. So over to you. You do. Alright, thank you. Morning. Morning. True.

And uh, good day. Good day. Uh, my name is Terrence Wood. I work for Australia National University, but I'm a New Zealander and I live here in New Zealand for about half of the year. When I'm not in New Zealand, I split my time between Canberra and Port Moresby. Um, I'm an academic, obviously my research focuses on foreign aid in the Pacific and also on the political economy of Western Pacific countries.

Um, so I'm gonna speak to you, uh, in my academic capacity today, but also unfortunately at one point this talk, I'm gonna lapse into my alternate persona, which is disgruntled taxpayer of Kilburn. Um, so only For half the Year though. Yes. Yeah, yeah. The other half of the year I'm quite happy a gentleman actually. Um, so we might be able to skip forward a few slides in the name of expediency.

The most important thing I want to tell you though is although New Zealand is a small donor globally, we focus a, a large amount of our aid on Pacific and compared to other regions globally, uh, the Pacific is the one part of the world where, uh, Pacific countries, uh, the aid in flows are very large compared to the size of most Pacific countries economies or in other, uh, words.

Um, New Zealand is one of the largest donors in the Pacific and the Pacific is one of the, uh, regions globally where aid really matters a lot. So if we deliver our aid well in the Pacific, we can do a lot to help the countries of the Pacific, but if we get it wrong, uh, well uh, best case scenario will be squandering. Our aid worst case scenario will actually be harming the countries who we are claim to be friends with in the region.

Um, and on that subject, on the subject of getting things wrong, I'm now going to explain to you why tying aid would be a bad thing. Um, do you all understand, uh, what I say when I'm, uh, using the term tying aid? Yeah, right. So I see you received the submission from the Pacific Exporters Network and uh, the speaker was uh, suggesting that 30% of New Zealand aid should be, uh, tied to private sector companies that work in the Pacific.

Um, and there are a number of reasons why tying our aid to the Pacific would be a bad idea. Um, and if we skip forward Zach to reason one, um, the first thing, and this is really important to understand, there are three different, uh, things that you can focus your aid on. You can focus your aid on helping poor people living in uh, poorer countries.

You can focus your aid on advancing your geostrategic interests and you can foc or you could focus your aid on helping subsidize your own private sector companies. And giving AID'S complex, it's really not that easy. So it turns out that typically you can only focus your aid on one of these three things. At least if you want your aid to achieve one of these objectives, it's very hard to do that object, uh, meet that objective successfully if you try and do more than one thing at once.

It's really a question of finding the right horse for the right course when you give your foreign aid. Um, and so give you a tangible example. Um, there are some things where you would really want an NGO to deliver, uh, the aid that we were giving. So if we were trying to tackle uh, something such as malaria and Papua New Guinea, you'd want a non-governmental organization that was uh, well versed in how to address issues associated with malaria.

You wouldn't want a private sector company from New Zealand. They wouldn't do the job well obviously. And on top of that, if you compelled in fact to give that contract to a private sector company, you probably wouldn't receive good value for money. So it's hard to give aid effectively. And my advice to you is that when you're trying to decide what you wanna do with your aid, you should really focus on delivering aid through the right horse for the right course, uh, where to do it, uh, to making your choices.

Um, and speaking about trying to get the right horse for the right course. Reason two, well if you ask New Zealanders what they would like their aid focused on, um, which we've done once in a public opinion survey, you'll find that the vast majority of New Zealanders say that they don't want their aid focused on advancing New Zealand's geostrategic interests or assisting New Zealand's businesses.

Uh, they want their aid focused on helping poor people in poorer countries and that would seem quite significant 'cause these are the taxpayers who fund our government aid program. So that's another reason why's Ty aid would be a bad thing to do. The third reason, and I saw this was in MFAs written response to some of your questions and is that tying aid violates international, uh, norms and it violates international agreements that we've been party to as a country.

Um, and that's the sort of thing that if we were to tie a large share of our aid, 30% of our aid was a suggestion that I think was provided by the Pacific Exporters network. That's the sort of thing that would make us look bad internationally. Now if you're a large country such as the United States or China, it's pretty easy to get away with looking bad internationally. But if you're a smaller country like New Zealand, you tend to live or die by your international reputation.

And certainly when it comes to our aid in the Pacific, well there's one country in particular who our international reputation matters a lot too. And that is of course Australia. Australia's by far and away the largest aid donor in the Pacific. They're also our most important strategic partner and they tie next to none of the aid they give to the Pacific.

Um, and if they were to notice that all of a sudden we were tying a large share, say 30% of the aid that we were to give to the region, they wouldn't be happy. They view us as an important partner. And if we were to tie a large share of our aid to a region where they would give most of their aid in an untied manner, they would start to think of us as a free rider, someone who is shirking our obligations, uh, to either advance our shared geo strategic interests in the region or to help promote development objectives in the region.

So it would make us look bad in the eyes of a country that matters a lot to us. The next reason why we w wouldn't want to tie our aid or why it be a bad idea to tie our aid to specifically mandate that a large share of our Agent Pacific was given via private sector organizations in New Zealand is that private sector organizations in New Zealand are already very effective at winning contracts for New Zealand's foreign aid to the Pacific that is delivered by uh, private sector organizations.

So currently about 10% of New Zealand New Zealand's aid Pacific goes through the private sector. That's actually a larger share of our aid, uh, uh, than we uh, uh, root through NGOs. So about 10% of our aid Pacific goes through the private sector and of that age, New Zealand private sector firms already win 70% of the contracts or nearly 70% of contracts.

So they win the lion's share of the contracts for private sector aid going through the Pacific. They do so 'cause they have a pretty significant home court advantage. They are getting a good chunk of our aid to the region without us having to stipulate that this aid needs to flow through them in an uncompetitive manner. And then the next reason, um, why giving aid or tying aid to the Pacific would be a bad idea, uh, is sort of, uh, set up in response to one reason why it might be a good idea.

So one reason why tying our aid to the Pacific might be a good idea is one might contend that tying our aid to the region would be a good way of helping New Zealand businesses and doing so in a manner that promoted broad-based economic growth in New Zealand. Unfortunately, that rationale was mistaken too. So if we were to slip forward a little bit, Zach to the uh, bar chart, uh, although you could probably all see it in front of you on your slides, if you want to grow our economy, uh, by engaging in industrial policy policies

that help our own domestic industries, what you really want to do is system and exporting to large global markets. And this chart is a bar chart. The height of each bar on the chart reflects the GDP of the country in question. Each bar is a country. The countries over that side of the chart are countries with very large economies, uh, China, United States, European countries, uh, Australia.

And those are the countries where you want to exporters to be focusing their attention. If we are successful in those economies, we will succeed in growing our own economy. At the other end of the chart, you'll see a bunch of countries with very small economies. They just aren't significant markets. Even if we are successful exporters to those countries, we're gonna do next to nothing to help our own economy.

Uh, what side of the chart are the Pacific countries on? Well, they're all over there. Their countries are labeled and as you can see, they're on the wrong side of the chart. So if we were to mandate that a certain share of our aid to the Pacific had to be flowed through New Zealand, New Zealand private sector countries, our companies would be doing next to nothing to help grow our economy, all we would be doing is subsidizing a small subset of our private sector companies.

All we'd be doing is subsidizing vested interests in New Zealand and that's not how it's meant to work here in New Zealand. We have a market economy. And uh, next slide Zach, because this is an important one. It's where I'm going to slip into my alternate, uh, persona, which is disgruntled taxpayer from Kilburn, right? In this current economic downturn, I have friends who run small businesses and who are tradespeople and they're suffering right now.

They're not finding it easy and no one's talking about subsidizing them. No one's talking about propping up their businesses. They are set to sink or swim by the state of our economy. If we were to tie our aid to the Pacific, we would be propping up or would be assisting the performance of small number of companies and doing so in a way that we don't do.

So for most private sector organizations in New Zealand, what's more would be assisting a small number of companies, often large engineering firms that will survive the current recession just fine. That isn't necessarily going to be the case for my friends who run small businesses. So why should I as a New Zealand taxpayer be subsidizing a small group of uh, organizations whilst at the same time seeing my own friends businesses suffer in their own economic fortunes, suffer as a result.

Then the final reason why tying aid to the Pacific would be a bad idea runs as follows. So along with most of my fellow New Zealand taxpayers, I support first and foremost focusing our aid on it, helping people, poorer people in poorer countries. However, you will find people in New Zealand's foreign policy establishment, people infa for example.

We think we should devote our aid on some other, um, for some on advancing another reason. And that reason is they would like to use our aid geo strategically, um, to advance our geostrategic interest and in particular in the Pacific to help chase China out of the region. Now, I don't agree with my friends at MFA about this and if you want we could bring me and emfa together one day to have a debate about it.

The main point I want to make today though is that if you tie New Zealand aid to New Zealand firms, you will undermine your ability to advance our geostrategic interests in the region. And the reason why you'll do this because it becomes pretty obvious to Pacific politicians and also people in Pacific countries when you're using your aid to try and advance your own economic interests.

And a really good example of a country which has done this terribly is in fact China itself. So when China has given aid to many Pacific countries, it has tied that aid to uh, its own state owned enterprises. And it is an often involved itself in large infrastructure projects and in the Western Pacific countries that I know best.

What that has led to has been uh, Chinese state owned enterprises going into those countries building large infrastructure projects. And when they've done this, the labor working on those projects has been uh, Chinese nationals. And this has caused real resentment amongst people living in the settlements of Hara or Port Mosby.

'cause a lot of those people are unemployed. They look at these large infrastructure projects and they say, well, why aren't we working on those projects? Why are Chinese nationals working on those projects? And that has really undermined China's ability to cultivate soft power in the Pacific. It's really undermined its ability to make it look good in the region. And if we were to try and emulate that in some sort of way, we'd find that we also undermined our own soft power.

So regardless if you're someone like me who just wants our aid to help people living in Pacific countries, or if you're someone, someone like some of the people in MFA who wants to use aid to advance our geo strategic interests, you're gonna find that tying that aid to our firms will undermine our objectives. That's all I've got to say for now. Excellent.

Um, please, uh, feel free to ask me any questions you might have. That's Very useful. Thanks Terrance. Appreciate that. Um, Tim Costley. Yeah, I'll just, can I just devil's advocate. Some would say, what's the point of us doing it if we are not getting the recognition for it? Like are we better to help at least some Kiwi businesses than some Chinese businesses? Wouldn't, wouldn't your mates rather that someone's getting a bit of help out of this in New Zealand? Right. New Zealand aid, which go to private sector organizations.
